course_title: Special Topics: Cross-listed: The Black Power Republic
subject: HIST
course_description: Selected topics in history for students in non-history originating (cross-listed) courses. May be repeated for credit when topic varies.
course_notes: (Same as AFS 389 4, AAS 385 6) This course offers a critical exploration of Liberia from past to present as one of the most unique political and cultural experiments in the modern Black world. Founded in the nineteenth century by formerly enslaved African Americans and free Black settlers, Liberia became the first modern African republic and a powerful symbol of Black self-governance, sovereignty, and possibility. Often celebrated, misunderstood, or overlooked, Liberia's history reveals complex questions about freedom, citizenship, identity, empire, and Pan-Africanism. Through historical study and contemporary analysis, students will examine Liberia's founding, settler-indigenous relations, state formation, economic development, and role in global Black politics. The course also explores the nation's civil wars, reconstruction, democratic transitions, migration, and present-day cultural and political life. Special attention will be given to Liberia's contributions to Black internationalism, anti-colonial thought, and movements for liberation across Africa and the diaspora. Drawing from history, political science, sociology, literature, film, and music, students will engage Liberia not as a footnote, but as a central site for understanding race, nationhood, and Black futures. By the end of the course, students will be able to critically assess Liberia's past, interpret its present, and imagine its place in global African futures.
